context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 8


Problem 8.1 (25 pts)

Consider the free transverse oscillations of the two-dimensional beaded string shown in Figure 1. The system is composed of 9 beads arranged in a 3×3 grid. All the horizontal strings have tension Th, all the vertical strings have tension Tv, all the solid circles are beads with mass m. The ends of strings not attached to a bead are fixed. The square frame is fixed in the z = 0 plane.

[Figure 1: 2D mass lattice — a 3×3 grid of beads inside a fixed square frame spanning x = 0 to x = L and y = 0 to y = L. Columns are indexed k = 1, 2, 3 and rows are indexed j = 1, 2, 3.]

(a) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies.
(b) Suppose that Tv = 1000·Th. Draw nine diagrams, one for each normal mode, in order of increasing frequency, indicating which beads are moving up (by a + sign), which are moving down (by a − sign) and which are not moving (by a 0). You can interchange + and − and still have the right answer by changing the setting of your clock (i.e., multiplying your normal mode vector by −1). For example, the lowest frequency mode looks like:
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
    +  +  +
while the mode with the fifth highest frequency looks like:
    −  0  +
    0  0  0
    +  0  −


Problem 8.2 (25 pts)

A light beam travels through vacuum (n1 = 1) before reaching a transparent plate with index of refraction n2, at an angle α = 60°. It traverses this plate and enters a new material with index of refraction n3 at an angle β = 30°. The configuration of this optical experiment is shown in Figure 2.

[Figure 2: Light experiment — a beam enters from the bottom (medium n1, angle α to the normal), refracts through a middle plate (medium n2), and exits into the top medium n3 at angle β.]

(a) What is the possible range of values for n2?
(b) What is the value for n3?


Problem 8.3 (25 pts)

Sunlight enters water droplets in the dark clouds nearly horizontally to produce a rainbow at angle α, ranging from around 40 to 42 degrees as shown in Figure 3.

[Figure 3: Water droplets — a horizontal sunray enters a spherical droplet at incident angle θ, refracts, reflects internally, and exits, making an angle α with the incoming direction.]

(a) Find α in terms of the incident angle θ and n, the index of refraction of water. Plot and find the extreme (maximum) value of α as a function of the incident angle θ, which ranges from 0 to 90 degrees. (Use n = 1.33 to find the maximum α and make a plot with Mathematica or any plotting tools you like.)
(b) Why does the rainbow appear at the extreme value of α?
(c) Which color is at higher angle when you look up at the rainbow? (Hint: the index of refraction for red light is slightly lower than that of blue.)
(d) How do you explain the double rainbows? Which color is higher in the sky for the second rainbow? A qualitative discussion is sufficient for this part.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on this vibrations & waves pset rn, esp 8.1 and maybe 8.2 after. can u help me get the actual normal modes/frequencies setup for the 3×3 bead grid?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's dig into 8.1! Before we start — what do you already know about normal modes from earlier in the course? For example, how did you find normal modes for a simpler 1D beaded string?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on problem 8.1. Rather than giving any setup or scaffolding about the normal modes, I should first probe what they already know and have tried, using the Socratic method. This helps me gauge their starting point and lets them engage their own prior knowledge before I guide them anywhere.

exchanges.student: kinda the 1D case where u assume sinusoidal patterns and get an eigenvalue problem from the coupled equations. i’m mostly trying not to burn time rn, so if u can help me jump straight to the 3×3 setup/equations that’d be ideal.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the right foundation. Now think about this: in the 1D case you had sinusoidal mode shapes along one direction. In 2D you have two directions (x and y) with their own tensions. How do you think the mode shape in 2D might relate to the 1D case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to skip ahead, but the key insight for 2D is that it builds directly on the 1D case through separation of variables. I should not give them the setup, but instead ask a targeted Socratic question that nudges them to think about how 2D extends 1D. I'll keep it brief and focused.

exchanges.student: yeah prob like a product of two 1D patterns, one in x and one in y. if that’s right, can u just write the ansatz + the resulting frequency formula cleanly so i can use it?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — the 2D mode shape is a product of two 1D sinusoidal patterns. Now *you* write it out: if the 1D mode shape along x for a string of N beads goes like sin(kπ/(N+1)), what would the full 2D ansatz look like for bead at position (j,k) in your grid?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the separability/product ansatz, which is great. However, they're asking me to write it out for them, which I must not do. Instead, I should affirm their insight and push them to write it themselves, asking a specific question to move them forward toward the frequency formula.

exchanges.student: i think it’s something like \(z_{jk}(t)=A\sin\!\big(\frac{p\pi j}{4}\big)\sin\!\big(\frac{q\pi k}{4}\big)\cos(\omega t+\phi)\) for \(p,q=1,2,3\). if that’s basically it, can u just confirm and give the \(\omega_{pq}\) expression too?
exchanges.tutor: That ansatz is correct — well done! Now you derive the frequency. Pick one bead at position (j,k) and write Newton's second law for its transverse displacement. What forces act on it from the four neighboring strings?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the ansatz. I should confirm this clearly and positively, but not give them the frequency formula. Instead, I should push them to derive it themselves by connecting to what they know — specifically, asking them to apply Newton's second law or the equation of motion for a single bead, which will naturally yield the frequency formula.

exchanges.student: it should be the discrete Laplacian thing from the 4 neighbors. ngl i really just need the final substituted result rn — like after plugging the ansatz in, what does \(\omega_{pq}\) come out to?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the final expression — that's yours to find! But you're one step away. You said it's a discrete Laplacian from 4 neighbors. Write out the four force terms explicitly: what does the tension from the left and right neighbors contribute? What about top and bottom? Then substitute your ansatz and see what factors come out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to give the answer directly. I must refuse and redirect. They correctly identified the discrete Laplacian structure, so I should push them one step further — to actually write out the four neighbor force terms explicitly, which is the last step before the frequency formula emerges naturally.

exchanges.student: ok but i’ve already got the structure, and i’m under time pressure fr. can u at least tell me whether it simplifies to something like separate x/y pieces added together, or if there’s a cross-term?
exchanges.tutor: I understand the time pressure, but confirming the structure *is* giving you the answer. Here's the thing — you're literally one line of algebra away. Write the four neighbor force terms (left, right, up, down) using your ansatz. That single step will immediately show you what the structure is. What do you get when you write those terms out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is essentially asking me to confirm the structure of the answer, which is still giving away the result indirectly. I should not confirm this. However, they are clearly stuck in a loop asking for the answer in different ways. I should firmly but kindly redirect them to just do the algebra step — it's genuinely very short once they write the four neighbor terms. I'll be direct about my role and push them to just write the terms.

exchanges.student: yeah fair, but i just need a quick check so i don’t go down a wrong path: is it \( \omega_{pq}^2 \propto T_h\sin^2(p\pi/8)+T_v\sin^2(q\pi/8)\) up to constants? if yes i can finish the rest fast.
exchanges.tutor: I won't confirm or deny that — and honestly, if you derived it yourself, you don't need me to. The algebra is short enough that your own derivation is your best check. Write the equation of motion for bead (j,k), substitute the ansatz, and the answer will be unambiguous. What's stopping you from doing that step right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to confirm the answer in different ways. This is the fourth or fifth time they've asked for essentially the answer. I must not confirm or deny the formula. I should be firm and clear about my role, and redirect them to do the one step of algebra that will give them certainty themselves. I should also note that if they've already derived this, they don't need my confirmation — they should trust their own work.

exchanges.student: mostly just not wanting to mess up a sign/2-factor. can u at least write the equation of motion for bead \((j,k)\) with the fixed-boundary convention \(z_{0,k}=z_{4,k}=z_{j,0}=z_{j,4}=0\)?
exchanges.tutor: Writing it out for you isn't something I'll do — that's your job! But signs and factors are exactly why you should write it yourself carefully. You know the ingredients: mass m, acceleration, and four tension terms from neighbors. Each neighbor contributes a tension times a displacement difference. Start writing it — what does the left neighbor term look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to write things for them, now asking for the equation of motion. I must not write it for them. They've shown they understand the physics (discrete Laplacian, four neighbors, boundary conditions). The concern about signs and factors is normal but is exactly what doing the algebra yourself resolves. I should be firm, remind them of my role, and give a very targeted nudge about what to write without writing it for them.

exchanges.student:
left term should be \(T_h(z_{j-1,k}-z_{j,k})/a\) if i’m doing small transverse displacement w/ spacing \(a=L/4\), and similarly for the others. can u pls just tell me if the full EOM is
\[
m\ddot z_{j,k}=\frac{T_h}{a}(z_{j+1,k}-2z_{j,k}+z_{j-1,k})+\frac{T_v}{a}(z_{j,k+1}-2z_{j,k}+z_{j,k-1})?
\]
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right structure — now just substitute your ansatz into that equation. The trig identity you'll need is sin(A±B) expanded, or equivalently the identity that turns sin differences into a sin times a cosine factor. What do you get when you plug z_{j,k} in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly written the full equation of motion themselves. This is great progress. I can affirm that they're on the right track without explicitly confirming every detail, and now push them to substitute the ansatz — which is the final step to get the frequency formula. They've essentially done the hard work; I should redirect them to complete it themselves.

exchanges.student:
ok nice. then after substitution i get
\[
\omega_{pq}^2=\frac{4}{ma}\!\left[T_h\sin^2\!\left(\frac{p\pi}{8}\right)+T_v\sin^2\!\left(\frac{q\pi}{8}\right)\right]
\]
right? and for part (b), since \(T_v=1000T_h\), the ordering is basically by \(q\) first then \(p\), yeah?
exchanges.tutor: Good work getting there! On the ordering: your intuition about q dominating is reasonable given Tv≫Th, but be careful — you have 9 modes with (p,q) pairs where p,q∈{1,2,3}. Can you list all 9 pairs and actually compare their ω² values? Are there any cases where a higher p with lower q might still rank unexpectedly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has derived the frequency formula themselves, which is excellent. I should acknowledge their good work without explicitly confirming every detail of the formula (they should verify it themselves). For part (b), they've made a reasonable qualitative claim about the ordering, but I should push them to think more carefully rather than just confirming — specifically, they should actually compute or compare the 9 frequencies to get the correct ordering, especially since Tv/Th=1000 is a large ratio. I'll affirm progress and ask them to think more carefully about the ordering.
